coredata.dc:title: Development of ultrathin polyamide nanofilm with enhanced inner-pore interconnectivity via graphene quantum dots-assembly intercalation for high-performance organic solvent nanofiltration
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Qin
Lin, Yuqing
Zhang, Pengfei

Jia, Yuandong
Istirokhatun, Titik

Matsuyama, Hideto
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a high-performance organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) membrane is engineered via nanoscale aminated graphene quantum dots (GQD_NH2)-mediated interfacial polymerization. The GQD_NH2, featuring favorable amine monomers, can atomically regulate the diffusion rate at the interface, yielding nanofilms with controllable thickness (decreasing from 200 to 100 nm). Furthermore, the zero-dimensional GQD_NH2 can serve as nanospacers to increase the inner-pore interconnectivity with loose internal architecture, while preserving the narrow free volume distribution via covalent interactions with the rubbery polymeric matrix. An improvement in solvent separation property is achieved, where the best-performing GQD_NH2@PA thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane reaches a magnitude of 2.6-fold higher than that of the pristine polyamide (PA) membrane without significantly compromising its rejection toward organic solvation macro/micromolecules solutes, exhibiting high solvent permeances for mild solvents (such as 11.1 L m−2 h−1·bar−1 for methanol) and aggressive solvents (such as 5.9 L m−2 h−1·bar−1 for dimethylformamide), performing a competitive perm-selectivity among the state-of-the-art OSN membranes. The long-term operations (140 h) demonstrated the excellent stability of the nanocomposite membranes. We herein report the development of TFN membrane with thin and loose architecture for highly efficient organic solvent-based separation process.

head.ce:title: Development of ultrathin polyamide nanofilm with enhanced inner-pore interconnectivity via graphene quantum dots-assembly intercalation for high-performance organic solvent nanofiltration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a high-performance organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) membrane is engineered via nanoscale aminated graphene quantum dots (GQD_NH2)-mediated interfacial polymerization. The GQD_NH2, featuring favorable amine monomers, can atomically regulate the diffusion rate at the interface, yielding nanofilms with controllable thickness (decreasing from 200 to 100 nm). Furthermore, the zero-dimensional GQD_NH2 can serve as nanospacers to increase the inner-pore interconnectivity with loose internal architecture, while preserving the narrow free volume distribution via covalent interactions with the rubbery polymeric matrix. An improvement in solvent separation property is achieved, where the best-performing GQD_NH2@PA thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane reaches a magnitude of 2.6-fold higher than that of the pristine polyamide (PA) membrane without significantly compromising its rejection toward organic solvation macro/micromolecules solutes, exhibiting high solvent permeances for mild solvents (such as 11.1 L m−2 h−1·bar−1 for methanol) and aggressive solvents (such as 5.9 L m−2 h−1·bar−1 for dimethylformamide), performing a competitive perm-selectivity among the state-of-the-art OSN membranes. The long-term operations (140 h) demonstrated the excellent stability of the nanocomposite membranes. We herein report the development of TFN membrane with thin and loose architecture for highly efficient organic solvent-based separation process.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid expansion of industrial separation processes, such as water purification, carbon capture, and organic solvent recovery that involves increased energy consumption and cost intensive, is a cause for concern. Membrane-based technology requires only one-tenth of the energy required to process the equivalent amount of liquid using conventional separation technologies (e.g., evaporation or distillation), potentially enabling processing within a reasonable time [1,2]. Therefore, to extend industrial applications, the development of high-performance membrane materials with ultrafast solvent permeability and high molecular selectivity in organic solvents of different polarities is of great fundamental and practical significance.

Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) is a promising technique that enables highly efficient separation toward small molecules (molecular weights ranging from 200 to 1000 Da) from organic liquids [3]. Most OSN membranes reported in the literature, as well as those used commercially, are thin-film composite (TFC) polyamide (PA) membranes prepared via interfacial polymerization, which involves irreversible crosslinking of two fast-reacting multifunctional monomers based on the Schotten-Baumann reaction [4]. However, a high crosslinking degree not only affords amplified chemical resistance in harsh solvents, but also causes an unavoidable decrease in membrane permeability, owing to the low free volume and large thickness of the selective nanofilm with enhanced mass transfer resistance. Furthermore, it is known that achieving exceedingly high permeability while maintaining high selectivity, is imperative for reaching high efficiency in membrane separation processes. Thus, the engineering of OSN membranes with high-performance overcoming the trade-off limits between permeability and selectivity is of primary importance, and two straightforward strategies have been proposed for this purpose. One is to develop TFC-OSN membranes with ultrathin selective layers [5]. Nevertheless, highly sophisticated manipulation or multistage procedures are required for the fabrication of ultrathin membranes, which may easily result in the formation of defects owing to their vulnerability to larger stress over a prolonged crossflow filtration process. The other is to tailor the intrinsic microstructures with interconnected microporosity inside the membrane bulk at the molecular level [6]. Numerous methods have been reported to date, and thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes embedding with nanomaterials (e.g., graphene oxide [7], carbon nanotubes [8], metal organic frameworks [9], and other organic/inorganic-based nanofillers [10–12]) into polymeric matrix have been fabricated. However, to realize the precise control of interfacial polymerization for the development of advanced TFN membranes is still incredibly difficult, owing to the fast, irreversible, and complicated reaction process. There are other bottleneck issues that remain to be addressed: 1) the poor interaction between the incorporated nanofillers and the PA matrix may result in the leakage of nanofillers during the filtration process, and thereby lose their dominant roles within TFN membranes over time [13]; 2) the unfavorable affinity of incorporated nanofillers toward the PA matrix without coordinating interactions may yield defective interface structures and nonselective voids [14]; 3) an improper size or structural morphology and inhomogeneous dispersion of nanofillers inside the polymeric matrix may lead to enhanced intrinsic resistance or defect-forming propensity; 4) the difficulty in the large-scale fabrication of nanofillers for practical applications of TFN membranes [15]. To address these issues, an appropriate selection and modification of nanofillers to be introduced in the selective layer is crucial. These nanofillers should afford the material functionalities to achieve an optimized transport path for rapid solvent transport, thus yielding the potential to surpass the permeability–selectivity upper-bound of the existing PA membranes.

The recent emergence of zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which constitute a novel class of graphene oxide-like materials composed of a one-atom-thick nanosheet (<2 nm) structure with extremely high edge-to-volume ratios, excellent chemical stability, low toxicity, and easy fabrication properties, has attracted tremendous research interest for TFN membrane fabrication [16–19]. Zheng et al. reported a TFN membrane that comprised GQDs decorated with a hyperbranched polyethyleneimine skin layer, whereof this membrane demonstrated a 2.8-fold enhancement in the water permeating flux with an increased Na2SO4 rejection ratio of 98.1% compared to that of the pristine PA TFC membrane [20]. Other literature has also reported that incorporating a certain amount of GQDs can aid the formation of a PA nanofilm with improved water permeance, owing to the decreased membrane thickness and loose internal architecture [21]. Although recent studies have indicated the quantum nanodots may increase the perm-selectivity of membranes, most of which focuses on the desalination-based rather than the organic solvent-based applications, thus the intrinsic “trade-off” limits between the two performance metrics of permeability and solute selectivity still remain challenges to surmount for OSN membrane developments. More importantly, fewer explanations have been proposed to elucidate the underlying mechanism of how and why the interior geometry and surface chemistry of the embedded quantum nanodots contribute to the initiation of interfacial polymerization at the molecular level. In this regard, a fundamental understanding of the role of embedded GQDs in constructing ultrathin and loose structured PA nanofilms is required for fabricating high-performance OSN membranes.

Herein, we report the fabrication of a highly permeable-selective composite OSN membrane via nano-size aminated graphene quantum dot (GQD_NH2)-mediated interfacial polymerization, as schematically shown in Fig. 1 b. During the interfacial polymerization process, the GQD_NH2 nanofillers bearing affinity groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino) enable favorable interactions with amine monomers, retarding their diffusion rates toward the organic phase and yielding ultrathin nanofilms with a controllable thickness of ~100 nm (Fig. 1a). Furthermore, the GQD_NH2 nanofillers incorporated into the PA matrix can act as nanospacers to construct the loosely structured nanofilm with enhanced inner-pore interconnectivity, while preserving its narrow free volume distribution owing to the introduced amino groups that react with the acyl chloride of 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride, thereby contributing to the perfect interfacial compatibility (Fig. 1c). The physicochemical properties of TFN membranes were systematically investigated by regulating the intercalation of surface amino decoration on GQDs. The solvent permeance of the resultant GQD_NH2 nanofiller-embedded polyamide (GQD_NH2@PA) membrane was significantly enhanced (e.g., 2.6-fold higher for methanol permeance) compared to that of the pristine PA membrane with uncompromised selectivity toward organic dyes in OSN separations. More importantly, the GQD_NH2@PA membrane exhibits great robustness toward salty, strong acid/alkali solutions, and harsh solvents (N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and methyl alcohol (MeOH)) over continuous filtrations for 140 h. Overall, this study demonstrates that our strategy for synthesizing the TFN membranes can provide a versatile and promising approach to advance the highly-performance organic solvent-based molecular separation applications.

Polyketone (PK, molecular weight (Mw) of 200,000 g mol−1) was procured from Asahi Kasei Company (Japan). citric acid (99%), MeOH (99.8%), n-hexane (99.0%), acetone (99.5%), tetrahydrofuran (THF, Guaranteed Reagent), ethanol (EtOH, 99.5%), N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAc, Wako Special Grade), NMP (Wako Special Grade), DMF (99.5%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.0%), triethylamine, sodium dodecyl sulfate, and ammonia solution (25%) were purchased from FUJIFILM Wako Pure Chemical Company. (Osaka, Japan). Diethylenetriamine, 1,3-phenylene diamine (MPD, 99.0%), resorcinol, isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 99.5%), 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC), methyl orange (MO, 327.3 g mol−1, 98%), Sudan black B (SBB, 456.6 g mol−1, 95%), Janus green B (JGB, 511.1 g mol−1, 95%), erythrosin B (EB, 836.2 g mol−1, 95%), Rose Bengal (RB, 1017.6 g mol−1, 95%), eosin Y (EY, 647.9 g mol−1, 95%), and 10-camphorsulfonic acid were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry (Tokyo, Japan). Dopamine, disperse orange 3 (DO3, 242.3 g mol−1, 95%), and reactive orange 16 (RO16, 617.5 g mol−1, 90%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pristine GQD and GQD_NH2 nanofillers
ce:section.ce:para: Pristine GQD nanofillers were fabricated as previously reported [12]: citric acid (50 wt%)-based aqueous solution (15 g) was prepared in a 50-mL beaker, followed by a microwave-assisted pyrolysis process for 200 s at 700 W. After cooling, the semi-finished products were re-dispersed in pure water for dialysis treatment for 3–4 days. The dilute GQD solution was collected and freeze-dried to obtain GQD powder for further use. The GQD_NH2 nanofillers were prepared by following the same procedure with that of the pristine GQD nanofillers, with the additional incorporation of certain amounts of diethylenetriamine (3.0 mol/L) to the citric acid-based aqueous solutions prior to the thermal treatment and a pyrolysis time of 300 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pristine PA, GQD@PA, and GQD_NH2@PA membranes
ce:section.ce:para: A porous polydopamine-decorated polyketone (PDA-d-PK) substrate was fabricated for the subsequent interfacial polymerization, because of the following reasons: 1) the PDA-d-PK possesses higher hydrophilicity and more ultrafine micro/nanospherical structures compared with the pristine PK substrate, of which these advantages may facilitate a more uniform distribution of MPD aqueous solution on the substrate surface when conducting interfacial polymerization [22]; 2) the surface PDA modification may also enhance the compatibility between the polyamide layer and PK substrate via the strong interfacial interactions of covalent bonds (between amino groups of PDA and acyl chloride of TMC) and hydrogen bonds, respectively [23,24]. An aqueous solution (100 mL) of MPD (0.0625 wt%) containing 10-camphorsulfonic acid (4 wt%), sodium dodecyl sulfate (0.25 wt%), and triethylamine (2 wt%) was first prepared under vigorous stirring for 30 min. After that, the as-prepared solution was poured onto the as-prepared PDA-d-PK substrate and soaked for 3 min, and then the extra MPD-based solution was removed using an air knife. Subsequently, the substrate was submerged in a TMC (0.0375 wt%)-based n-hexane solution for another 2 min, and then treated by a heat curing process for 5 min at 100 °C. Finally, the as-prepared PA TFC membrane was soaked in pure water (4 °C) to hydrolize the excess acyl chlorides for further study.

The GQD-embedded PA TFN membranes were fabricated using the same procedure as the above mentioned interfacial polymerization method using the same PDA-d-PK substrate. Specific concentrations of GQD and GQD_NH2 particles were dispersed in the MPD-based aqueous solution for the subsequent preparation of GQD@PA and GQD_NH2@PA TFN membranes, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment of the diffusion of MPD from the aqueous phase to the organic phase
ce:section.ce:para: First, a homogeneous aqueous phase (30 mL) with GQDs (0.6 wt%) and MPD (0.4 wt%) was poured into a 100-mL beaker, and then n-hexane (20 mL) was carefully injected into the aqueous phase. After 120 s, n-hexane (2 mL) containing MPD was removed from the n-hexane–air interface and was placed into quartz cuvettes. The absorbance variation of MPD in n-hexane was measured by ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, whereof the MPD shows a maximum absorption peak at approximately 252.1 nm. The variation in the absorbance of MPD with time was measured from fixed-wavelength UV–vis absorption spectra [25]. 1 mL of 0.4 wt% MPD aqueous solution (with or without the addition of 0.6 wt% GQDs) was slowly added into an approximately 3-mL quartz cuvette. The height of the aqueous phase remained below the light path of the spectrometer. To form an interface, the n-hexane (1.5 mL) was gently injected on top of the aqueous phase within 15 s. The amount of diffused MPD in the n-hexane phase from 15 s to 120 s was calculated from UV–vis absorption spectra.

The diffusion coefficients of MPD in GQD and GQD_NH2 D2O solutions were measured and recorded by two-dimensional diffusion-ordered (2D DOSY) NMR spectroscopy carried out at 25 °C [26]. The GQDs and MPD concentrations in these measurements were 0.6 wt% and 0.4 wt%, respectively.

The chemical composition and chemical bonding of the GQD and GQD_NH2 nanoparticles were characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The morphologies of the GQD-based nanoparticles were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Powder crystallizations of the GQD-based nanoparticles were obtained by the X-ray diffraction (XRD). The microstructures of the pristine PA, GQD@PA, and GQD_NH2@PA membranes were examined by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). A thin layer of osmium tetroxide was sputter-coated onto the surface of the samples prior to characterization. Cross-sectional images of the membranes were observed by TEM. The surface morphologies (roughness) of the membranes were characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM). Water contact angle measurements were conducted using a goniometer. The pore size/pore size distributions and free volumes of the membranes were measured by positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS), and the slow positron beams were generated from a 50 mCi (2005) Na source.

Pure solvent permeances, including those of n-hexane, IPA, DMAc, NMP, DMSO, EtOH, THF, DMF, acetone, and MeOH, were investigated. A feed solution of 20 ppm was prepared for the NMP, DMF, and MeOH-based solvents for the dye filtration experiments. All the filtration tests were conducted under the same transmembrane pressure of 10 bar and pre-compacted at the same pressure for 60 min until a stable permeating flux was achieved. The solvent permeability (P) of the GQD-based membranes was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
P =
Δ V
Δ P × Δ t × A
where ΔV is the volume of the collected permeate (L) during a certain period (Δt; h), ΔP is the applied transmembrane pressure between the feed and permeate sides (bar), and A is the effective surface area (m2) of the membrane.

The dye rejection ratio (R) was calculated using the equation:
(2)
R =
(
1 −
C p
/
C f
)
× 100 %
where C p and C f are the NaCl concentrations of the permeate and feed solutions, respectively. The dye concentrations were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (V-650KE, JASCO Company, Japan).

To evaluate the long-term solvent resistance of the pristine PA and the newly developed GQD_NH2@PA membranes, separation tests were conducted using MeOH, NMP, and DMF with 20 ppm RB as the feed solution, under a transmembrane pressure of 10 bar over a prolonged testing time of 140 h.

The GQDs were fabricated via a facile method by directly pyrolyzing abundant and renewable nature-based citric acid with/without the surface decorating agent DETA. After dialysis of the resulting products, the homogeneous GQD suspension obtained was bright and flavescent under 365-nm UV–vis irradiation (Fig. S1). This suspension remained stable even after storage for more than 1 month, indicating its superior stability and aqueous dispersibility. The TEM image of pristine GQD nanofillers showed a zero-dimensional nanostructure with an average lateral diameter of ~10 nm (Fig. S2a), whereas the GQD_NH2 nanofillers displayed an enlarged profile of nearly ~25 nm (Fig. S2b). The larger aggregates could be explained by the stronger interactions owing to the intensified hydrogen bonding derived from the amino groups [19]. All the high-resolution (HR) TEM images of GQDs exhibit a similar crystal lattice fringes similar to those of the graphene oxide based materials [27,28]. This result indicates that the intrinsic crystallization of GQDs would be retained after the surface amino decoration, as demonstrated by the similar XRD patterns with the similar crystalline band at ~19° [27,29] (Fig. S3). Both the samples displayed the broad diffraction peak, which could be attributed to the small particle size of GQD and GQD_NH2 [30] and the turbostratic carbon phase [31]. The interlayer spacing (d(002), Table S1) of the resulting sample were 0.47 nm calculated according to Bragg's law, and these results were in accordance with the reported literature [12]. The AFM results (Figs. S2c and S2d) showed that both GQD- and GQD_NH2-based nanofillers have uniform nanoscale roughness profiles of 8 and 15 nm, respectively, indicating their successful fabrication.

The surface functionalization of amino (–NH/–NH2) groups onto the resultant GQDs and their retained chemical structures/compositions were further confirmed by FTIR and XPS characterizations. Fig. 2 a shows that both GQD and GQD_NH2 nanofillers give similar FTIR spectra, where the broad peak located at ~3300 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups (–OH), and the scissoring peaks located at 1700 cm−1 are ascribed to the asymmetrical/symmetrical vibrations of carbonyl groups (–CO) from carboxyl groups [32]. Additional peaks of GQD_NH2 nanofiller, located at approximately 3000, 1600, and 1300 cm−1, could be attributed to the stretching vibrations of amino (N–H), carbonyl groups (–CO) from amide groups, and amide (C–N) groups, respectively [33], indicating the successful surface functionalization of GQDs via microwave-assisted amidation. The chemical structures and compositions of the synthesized GQDs were investigated by XPS measurements. The wide-scan XPS spectra (Fig. 2b) of the GQD-based nanofillers exhibit only two peaks representing C (285 eV) and O (532 eV), whereas that of the GQD_NH2 reveals an additional characteristic peak of N (400 eV) [27,34]. The analysis of core-level C 1s and N 1s spectra revealed a plausible combination of different types of GQD-based nanofillers before and after surface functionalization (Fig. 2c–d, respectively). For GQD_NH2, an increase in the C–N (C–NH, C–N–CO, and C–NH2 + in C 1s) groups was observed compared to the pristine GQDs without the C–N characteristic peak [35]. The N 1s spectra confirmed the presence of active amino groups (–NH2) at the edge of GQD_NH2 [36], which would be responsible for interfacial polymerization by covalent bonding with acyl chloride inside the polyamide selective layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of GQDs@PA-based TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To overcome the trade-off limits between the permeability and selectivity of membrane separation performance, GQD-based nanofillers before and after amino group decoration were incorporated into the PA layer via interfacial polymerization. A highly porous thin polyamide layer (thickness sub-100 nm) was fabricated by controlling the diffusion rate of MPD in the aqueous phase toward an n-hexane phase containing TMC, in the presence of GQDs [26]. Fig. 3 a and b (the UV–vis spectra of MPD) show that the GQDs or GQD_NH2 inhibit the rate-limiting MPD diffusion toward the organic phase because of their favorable affinity groups (–COOH, –NH/–NH2), which retard their quick diffusion via strong interactions with MPD molecules. Furthermore, the GQDs or GQD_NH2 may also exert steric hindrance against MPD molecules, leading to relatively restricted diffusion rather than rapid free diffusion behavior, as observed in 2D DOSY NMR spectroscopy results (Fig. 3c and Fig. S4). Remarkably, the restriction effect of MPD exerted by GQD_NH2 is more significant than that by GQDs. This is possible because the restriction effect is mainly derived from physical obstruction and chemical interaction, wherein the GQD_NH2 exhibits a larger size and a more favorable chemical affinity (–NH/–NH2) toward MPD molecules, which will be more advantageous to the diffusion-driven instability, generating even thinner and more roughened nanofilms.

The surface morphologies of pristine PA and GQDs@PA-based TFN membranes were investigated by FESEM, AFM, and TEM (Fig. 4 ). The top-view FESEM image of the PDA-d-PK substrate shows hierarchical and irregular macroporous structures in the range 200–500 nm (Fig. S5). After interfacial polymerization, all the macropores were homogeneously distributed upon their submersion in MPD/TMC solutions owing to the positive capillary forces, followed by the formation of a relatively smooth and continuous defect-free layer on the substrate surface (Fig. 4a). However, the GQDs@PA-based TFN membranes display a more significantly crumpled surface morphology with more prominent Turing-type and flower-like protuberances. Furthermore, the GQD@PA and GQD_NH2@PA membranes exhibited average roughness profile (Ra) of 155.2 and 183.4 nm, respectively (Table S2), which were higher than that of the pristine PA membrane (102.1 nm) (Fig. 4b, e, and 4h). Since the interfacial polymerization is a diffusion-governed process, we postulated that GQD-based nanofillers with desirable chemical and dimensional properties can regulate the transport of MPD by exerting a restriction effect on diffusion toward the organic phase [26] and this phenomenon has also been demonstrated in Fig. 3. Therefore, the formation of rougher surface profiles of the GQD@PA and GQD_NH2@PA TFN membranes may be due to the different growth directions of polyamide clusters and the lateral diffusion of MPD absorbed by GQD-based nanofillers, which contrasts with the relatively smooth surface of pristine PA originating from the uniform interfacial polymerization reaction. The GQD_NH2@PA TFN membrane revealed a much rougher surface microstructure. This is probably because the GQD_NH2 nanofillers with more active sites of amino groups would be more prone to react with the acyl chloride of TMC to form initial amide-tuft structures in their vicinity, resulting in the development of more nodular-profile architectures. Based on the knowledge that solvent permeance is proportional to the membrane's effective filtration area, such nanoscale stripped structures of GQD@PA and GQD_NH2@PA TFN membranes with high specific areas are potentially conducive to the enhancement of solvent transport.

The thickness and intrinsic porosity (free volume) of the PA film play key roles in the permeating flux of TFC/TFN membranes, and their roles were investigated by imaging the cross-sectional microstructures, as shown in Fig. 4c, f, and 4i. From these images, it is evident that both the GQD-embedded PA TFN membranes displayed an ultrathin microstructure (thickness sub-100 nm) with intrinsic inner-pore interconnectivity architecture compared to the relatively denser 250-nm-thick pristine PA membrane. This is because the GQD-based nanofillers retard the diffusion rate of MPD molecules toward TFC, owing to the stronger restriction effects with intenser steric hindrance and chemical interactions between the embedded nanofillers and MPD [21,26]. The formation of internal voids inside the PA film can be mainly attributed to the embedment of hydrophilic GQD-based nanofillers, in which the absorbed water tends to hydrolyze the acyl chloride to carboxyl groups and prevent the formation of polyamide in their vicinity [11,14]. Consequently, the incorporation of GQD-based nanofillers into the PA matrix simultaneously realized the two-in-one strategy by reducing the diffusion of MPD, thus generating a thinner polyamide film (the trend agrees well with the Freger equation [26,37]) and creating a loose microstructure with high free volume that may facilitate the quick transport of solvent molecules [26,38–40].

The chemical compositions and chemical properties of the newly developed selective layers were further investigated. The FTIR spectra (Fig. S6) shows the preliminary analysis results of pristine membranes with GQD-based nanofillers embedded inside the polymer matrix. The strong vibration bands peak at ~1700 cm−1 for all membranes, corresponding to the carbonyl (CO) stretching. Furthermore, the other characteristic peaks located at ~1300 and 3200 cm−1 represent the C–N and N–H stretching vibrations, respectively, originating from the primary aliphatic amines of the PA matrix [41]. The full-scan XPS spectra of the GQD_NH2@PA membrane (Fig. S7a) reveals an intensified N 1s peak compared to those of the pristine PA and GQD@PA membranes, probably due to the reaction of amino groups on GQD_NH2 with the acyl chloride of TMC to form amide bonds. The appearance of C–N (285.3 eV), C–N (399.4 eV), and N–H (401.2 eV) bonds [41,42] with intensified peaks in the high-resolution narrow scan spectra of C 1s (Fig. S7b) and N 1s (Fig. S7c) further confirmed the changes in the surface chemistry after the incorporation of GQD_NH2 nanofillers. The surface elemental compositions of the PA-based membranes are summarized in Table S3. A clear increase in the O/N elemental ratio of the GQD_NH2@PA membranes is observed, which suggests a reduced crosslinking degree with a more porous microstructure compared to that of the pristine PA membrane. This can be explained by the embedment of hydrophilic GQD-based nanofillers, which may interrupt the normal interfacial polymerization around the GQD nanofiller surfaces [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of GQDs@PA-based TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Conventional polymeric materials demonstrate inferior solvent resistance in aggressive organic solvents owing to their high solubility. Nevertheless, the highly crosslinked PA-based membranes were found to be effective in harsh organic solvents by sacrificing their permeability. In this study, the PDA-d-PK substrate exhibited solvent permeances toward n-hexane, NMP, DMF, and MeOH of 590.9, 61.2, 167.2, and 511.2 L m−2 h−1·bar−1, respectively (Fig. S8), owing to its high porosity with low intrinsic resistance. By taking advantage of the PDA-d-PK substrate, the OSN separation performance of PA-based TFC/TFN membranes was systematically evaluated by measuring the solvent permeance and macro/micro-molecular (dye) rejection. The pristine PA membrane showed the lowest solvent permeances of 0.7, 2.9, and 4.3 L m−2 h−1·bar−1 for NMP, DMF, and MeOH, respectively (Fig. 5 a), and these results were in accordance with those conventional membranes reported in most studies [43–46].

In comparison, both the TFN membranes performed relatively more rapid molecular transport of polar solvents (DMF, NMP, and MeOH), reaching up to 14.6 and 11.1 L m−2 h−1·bar−1 for MeOH using the GQD@PA and GQD_NH2@PA membranes (approximately 3.4 and 2.6 times higher, respectively, than that of the pristine PA TFC membrane), respectively. In contrast, the GQD-based nanofiller-embedded TFN membranes exhibited a lower permeating flux toward nonpolar solvents like n-hexane than that of the pristine TFC membrane, which can be attributed to the enhanced internal polarity (Fig. S9), owing to the incorporation of hydrophilic GQD/GQD_NH2 nanofillers [47,48]. The separation capabilities of the PA-based TFN membranes were measured by separating four individual dyes with different molecular weights dispersed in MeOH (Fig. 5b). Both the pristine PA and GQD_NH2@PA membranes demonstrated a nearly complete rejection of the large molecular-sized dyes, EY (647.9 g mol−1) and RB (1017.6 g mol−1). This result indicates the excellent compatibility between the GQD_NH2 and polyamide without large defects formed, as well as the desirable surface structural integrity of the pristine PA membrane. Notably, nearly 95% of the dyes MO (327.3 g mol−1) and SBB (456.6 g mol−1) with smaller molecular weights were rejected by the pristine PA and GQD_NH2@PA membranes, demonstrating the integrities of their selective layers within the separation range (200–1000 Da) of OSN membrane [3]. In comparison, the GQD@PA membrane displayed relatively poor rejection ratios compared to that of the GQD_NH2@PA membrane. This can be attributed to the unfavorable structural defects created at the polymer-filler interfaces, due to simple blending without any chemical interactions between the GQDs and the polyamide matrix. Consequently, the GQD_NH2@PA membrane was selected for further study.

The free volume characteristics of the PA TFC and GQD_NH2@PA TFN membranes measured by PALS are shown in Fig. 5c. By implanting positrons of moderate energy in the specimens, the depth profiles of fractional free volumes/free volume distributions of the PA active layers were obtained simultaneously. From the spectra characterized by the S parameter, it is evident that the incorporation of GQD_NH2 nanofillers may lead to an enlarged free volume cavity inside the PA matrix, inducing a change from the dense structure of pristine PA to a porous GQD_NH2@PA membrane. Further evidence is obtained from the calculated free volume distributions of the PA active layers (the inset of Fig. 5c), which indicates that the incorporation of GQD_NH2 may yield enlarged pore structures instead of merely shifting the pore size distribution to the larger-sized range without significantly changing the most effective pore size (increasing from 0.33 to 0.36 nm). These results are in accordance with those obtained by the rejection of macro/micromolecules (Fig. 5b), further confirming the formation of loose nanofilms with the embedment of GQD_NH2 nanofillers.

The separation property of the GQD_NH2@PA membrane was examined in different harsh organic solvents using solvent-based dye solutions. Fig. S10 shows that the GQD_NH2@PA membrane performs better separation properties in both NMP and MeOH, with lower molecular weight cut-off values below 327.3 g mol−1, whereas it exhibits a slightly higher molecular weight cut-off value in DMF. This indicates that the newly developed GQD_NH2@PA membrane suffered from swelling in DMF, which could be attributed to the swelling of the polymeric network and the selective dissolution of the loose polyamide microstructure with a low crosslinking degree [49,50]. The membrane selectivity with different loading masses of the GQD_NH2 nanofiller was systematically investigated (Fig. S11). The MeOH permeance increased while the MO rejection ratios remained nearly constant as the amount of GQD_NH2 nanofillers arisen from 0 to 0.6 wt%. The remarkable increase in separation property after the embedding of GQD_NH2 nanofillers can be ascribed to the following reasons: 1) formation of a loose polyamide membrane with enhanced inner-pore interconnectivity in the hybrid structure by generating interfacial voids with lower intrinsic resistance, which can act as highways for the quick transport of solvent molecules [11,12]; 2) relatively thinner membrane (with a controllable thickness of sub-100 nm) originating from the substantially reduced diffusion rate of amine monomers toward the organic phase can shorten the pathways for solvent passage [26,47]; 3) more prominent Turing-type and flower-like spatial structure originates from the embedment of GQD_NH2 nanofillers and can provide increased specific surface area for more solvent molecules to permeate through [51]; and 4) embedded GQD_NH2 nanofillers with enhanced surface hydrophilicity can amplify the affinity toward polar solvent molecules (exhibiting a lower contact angle than that of the pristine PA), prompting their adsorption on membrane surfaces with strengthened infiltrating property [47,48]. However, by continuously increasing the loading mass of the GQD_NH2 nanofillers to 1.2 wt%, a significant increase in solvent permeance (up to 14.1 L m−2 h−1·bar−1) was observed, along with a compromise in membrane selectivity (MO rejection ratio below 87.4%). This can be ascribed to the presence of large aggregates of GQD_NH2 nanofillers with a heterogeneous distribution inside the PA matrix, which may lead to the formation of less-selective pathways for the permeation of the solvent molecules, resulting in the leakage of dye solutes. In conclusion, a balance between the solvent permeances and solute rejections is needed to achieve superior separation properties. Therefore, the addition of 0.6 wt% of GQD_NH2 nanofillers was selected for further study.

To further investigate the molecular-scale transportation behavior of PA-based TFC/TFN membranes in various solvents, the relationships between solvent permeance and the reciprocal of physical parameters (Table S4), such as solubility (δp, Pa0.5), viscosity (η, Pa·s), and molar diameter (dm, m), were studied. As shown in Fig. S12, a roughly linear relationship can be observed for both PA-based TFC and TFN membranes [5], which is given by the equation:
(3)

K 1
×
δ p
)
/
(

d m 2
)
where K1 is the proportionality constant (m3·Pa−0.5). These results suggest that the molecular transport behavior can be mainly governed by the solution-diffusion theory [5,52]: molecules should be dissolved into the membrane surface first, and then diffuse through the membrane matrix, with solvent solubility and molar diameter playing important roles in their molecular passage through the PA film. The solvent viscosity (pore-flow module) also influences solvent permeation to some extent. These results are in accordance with those obtained with most reported MPD-based films [5,43,53]. According to these results, the smaller molecules of acetone (dm = 0.62 nm) and methanol (dm = 0.51 nm), with lower viscosities of 0.29 × 10−3 and 0.49 × 10−3 Pa S, respectively, reached the highest solvent permeances of up to 10.7 and 11.1 L m−2 h−1·bar−1, respectively. In addition, the newly developed GQD_NH2@PA membrane exhibited higher permeating capability for DMF, high permeance for THF and DMAc, and relatively lower permeance for DMSO, EtOH, NMP, and IPA because of their relatively larger molecular volumes and higher viscosities [53]. Notably, the GQD_NH2@PA membrane demonstrated higher permeances than those of the pristine PA for all organic solvents, which can be explained by its ultrathin nature and loose architecture with a rough surface profile, efficiently facilitating the passage of solvent molecules through the selective nanofilm.

The rejection behavior of the GQD_NH2@PA membrane toward different dye molecules (Table S5) in a mild MeOH solvent was systematically investigated, as shown in Fig. S13 and Fig. S14. These results suggested that the rejection ratio increased with increasing dye molecular weights/sizes. Furthermore, the newly proposed TFN membrane performed a good rejection (>95%) toward the negatively charged (MO (327.3 g mol−1), RO16 (617.5 g mol−1), and RB (1017.6 g mol−1)), positively charged (SBB (456.6 g mol−1) and JGB (511.1 g mol−1)), and neutral dyes (EY, 647.9 g mol−1) and EB (879.9 g mol−1)), indicating that the rejection mechanism of the GQD_NH2@PA membrane used in OSN applications mainly relies on the pore-size exclusion principle [53]. Furthermore, the proposed membrane exhibited a relatively low rejection ratio of 75.6% toward DO3 (242.2 g mol−1) and a relatively high rejection ratio of 95.3% toward MO (327.3 g mol−1), indicating a molecular weight cut-off of approximately 300 g mol−1 in MeOH. An extensive comparison between the GQD_NH2@PA membrane and the other reported nanofiller-based OSN membranes (MOFs [45,54,55] and ZIFs [54,56,57]) tested in MeOH for dye separation was conducted. Fig. 5d and Table S6 show that most TFC/TFN membranes were restricted by the “trade-off” between the permeance and molecular rejection, owing to the tightly structured selective layer with high crosslinking degrees [46], or interfacial defects that originate from the incompatibility between the larger-sized MOF materials or other inorganic nanoparticles and the polymer matrix [54]. However, the newly developed GQD_NH2@PA membrane showed a competitive solvent permeance and higher molecular rejection compared to other recently reported OSN membranes [54–57]. The improved separation performance of the newly developed GQD_NH2@PA membrane is mainly ascribed to the multiple integrations of the loose microstructure, ultrathin structure, crumpled surface architecture, and perfect interfacial compatibility between the incorporated nanofillers and the polymeric matrix [26].

Apart from the superior separation property, the GQD_NH2@PA membrane exhibited good operational stability under harsh environments. Fig. S15 shows that the GQD_NH2@PA membrane maintained a nearly constant MeOH permeance and MO rejection without a significant decline (below 0.5%) after immersion in acidic (HCl), alkaline (NaOH), and aqueous salty (NaCl) environments for 12 h, indicating its good structural stability.

Furthermore, the GQD_NH2@PA membrane demonstrated good separation properties in long-term filtration experiments, wherein a steady permeance of 10.8 L m−2 h−1·bar−1 was obtained, with almost total rejection (>99%) of RB in a mild MeOH solution over a prolonged filtration period of 140 h (Fig. 6 a), which indicates a long enough pre-filtration process (under the pressure of 10 bar integrated with 1 h) for the subsequent long-term filtration operation tests. At the same time, the GQD_NH2@PA membrane exhibited good resistance in aggressive organic solvents, with high permeances of 1.7 and 5.7 L m−2 h−1·bar−1 for NMP (Fig. 6b) and DMF (Fig. 6c) solutions, respectively, without significantly compromising on the permeating capability. These permeances are more than two-times higher than that of the pristine PA membrane over the continuous 140-h operation. Overall, this study highlights the superiority of the newly developed GQD_NH2@PA membrane with high separation properties and strong stability, thereby holding promise for practical industrial applications [53,58].

In this study, a high-performance thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane comprising nanosized aminated graphene quantum dots (GQD_NH2) incorporated into polyamide (PA) was fabricated via interfacial polymerization. An improvement in solvent permeability was achieved, with the permeability reaching a magnitude 2–3 folds higher than that of the pristine PA membrane without sacrificing its selectivity toward organic dyes. The fabricated TFN membrane exhibited high solvent permeances for mild polar solvents (11.1 L m−2 h−1·bar−1 for methanol) and aggressive organic solvents (5.9 L m−2 h−1·bar−1 for dimethyl formamide). This can be ascribed to the synergistic effects of the GQD_NH2 nanofillers: 1) featuring favorable chemical interactions with amine monomers, exerting a restriction on the diffusion rate of MPD molecules toward the organic phase, thus generating nanofilms of controlled thicknesses (decreasing from 250 nm to 100 nm); and 2) the nanosized GQD_NH2 can serve as nanospacers to increase the looseness of the internal architecture with high inner-pore interconnectivity, while preserving the narrow free volume distribution via covalent interactions between the nanofillers and the rubbery polymer. The GQD_NH2@PA membrane also exhibited a satisfactory stability in challenging operational environments, such as in salt solutions, strong acid/alkali solutions, and various solvent solutions. Overall, this study provides a potential candidate for high-performance OSN membranes that can overcome the conventional permeability–selectivity trade-off limits, further enabling separations in other organic liquid purification (solvent recovery, solute concentration, and catalytic process), or water treatment applications.

Yuqing Lin, Kecheng Guan, and Hideto Matsuyama convinced this project. Qin Shen, Pengfei Zhang, Jumpei Segawa, and Yuandong Jia conducted the experiments and analyzed the data. Qin Shen, Titik Istirokhatun, and Xingzhong Cao performed the characterizations. Yuqing Lin and Qin Shen wrote and edited the manuscript. All authors discussed the results and wrote the manuscript.
